critic_raw: The unrolled network phase retrieval approach has several methodological issues. First, the claim that the learned reference generalizes to unseen data distributions is evaluated on a limited set of test conditions; the paper does not specify the statistical distance between training and test distributions, making the generalization claim hard to evaluate rigorously. Second, the iterative unrolling approach inherits the computational cost of the underlying phase retrieval iterations while adding learned parameters—the paper does not provide a systematic comparison of computation time versus reconstruction quality tradeoffs against pure algorithmic baselines. Third, the robustness claim regarding spatial augmentations (shifting and rotation) is tested on a narrow set of augmentation types and magnitudes; extreme shifts or rotations may degrade performance, and the boundary conditions are not analyzed. Fourth, the use of a single reference image introduces a single-point-of-failure: the quality of reconstruction depends heavily on how well the reference captures the signal's statistics, yet the paper provides limited analysis of reference quality sensitivity. Fifth, the evaluation is performed on open-access datasets without cross-dataset validation, raising questions about whether reported performance metrics reflect genuine generalization or dataset-specific fitting.
all_issues_raised: Generalization to unseen distributions lacks statistical characterization of distribution shift
Computation-quality tradeoff versus algorithmic baselines not systematically compared
Robustness to spatial augmentations tested on narrow range of magnitudes without boundary analysis
Sensitivity to reference image quality is underanalyzed
Single-dataset evaluation limits evidence of true generalization

round_transcripts.critic: The unrolled network phase retrieval approach has several methodological issues. First, the claim that the learned reference generalizes to unseen data distributions is evaluated on a limited set of test conditions; the paper does not specify the statistical distance between training and test distributions, making the generalization claim hard to evaluate rigorously. Second, the iterative unrolling approach inherits the computational cost of the underlying phase retrieval iterations while adding learned parameters—the paper does not provide a systematic comparison of computation time versus reconstruction quality tradeoffs against pure algorithmic baselines. Third, the robustness claim regarding spatial augmentations (shifting and rotation) is tested on a narrow set of augmentation types and magnitudes; extreme shifts or rotations may degrade performance, and the boundary conditions are not analyzed. Fourth, the use of a single reference image introduces a single-point-of-failure: the quality of reconstruction depends heavily on how well the reference captures the signal's statistics, yet the paper provides limited analysis of reference quality sensitivity. Fifth, the evaluation is performed on open-access datasets without cross-dataset validation, raising questions about whether reported performance metrics reflect genuine generalization or dataset-specific fitting.
round_transcripts.defender: The generalization claim is supported by training on one dataset and testing on a held-out set drawn from a different distribution—the paper explicitly tests on out-of-distribution natural images not seen during training. The computational comparison is addressed through FLOP counts and runtime tables comparing the unrolled method to FISTA and other iterative baselines; the key finding is that fewer iterations are needed at inference time because the learned reference warm-starts the reconstruction. The augmentation robustness tests cover translation shifts up to 25% of image width and rotations up to 45 degrees, which are substantial ranges covering practical deployment conditions. Reference quality sensitivity is tested through experiments with random, learned, and oracle references showing that the learned reference substantially outperforms random while approaching oracle performance. The single-dataset concern is partially valid—the paper uses CelebA and MNIST as evaluation sets, providing two distinct domains.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain unresolved. First, while two datasets are used, CelebA and MNIST represent very different domains but both are well-studied benchmarks; the statistical characterization of train/test distribution shift is still absent. Claiming generalization without measuring the gap between distributions is a qualitative assertion. Second, the reference quality sensitivity analysis compares random versus learned versus oracle, but does not analyze degraded or adversarially chosen references—understanding the failure modes of reference selection is important for practical deployment where reference selection may not be controlled.
round_transcripts.defender: The two-dataset evaluation spanning continuous natural images (CelebA) and discrete digit images (MNIST) provides meaningful evidence of generalization across structurally different image statistics—quantifying the distributional gap precisely is not standard practice in the phase retrieval literature and is not required to validate the core contribution. The reference sensitivity analysis is complete within the paper's scope: random reference establishes a lower bound, oracle establishes an upper bound, and the learned reference is shown to be near-oracle. Adversarially constructed references are an edge case beyond the paper's stated problem setting. Both remaining critiques represent scope extensions rather than methodological flaws in the reported experiments.

all_issues_adjudicated: Generalization claim lacks statistical characterization of train-to-test distribution shift
Reference quality failure modes and adversarial reference sensitivity unanalyzed
